aliases: Sauce Beef Chicken Feet Lotus Root Kohlrabi Peanuts Platter

english: Sauce Beef Chicken Feet Lotus Root Kohlrabi Peanuts Platter

ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (Mixed dishes) is the closest parent. No specific code exists for a Beijing cold assorted platter.

serving.natural_language_portion: One set platter of approximately 400 g shared among 2–4 people, comprising five cold dishes: sauce beef, spicy chicken feet, lotus root, kohlrabi, and seasoned peanuts.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central component analogues: Beef braised (NDB 13365), Chicken feet cooked (NDB 05358), Lotus root raw (NDB 11224), Kohlrabi raw (NDB 11241), Peanuts dry roasted (NDB 16090)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (Foundation Foods / SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for this specific 5-component Beijing cold platter. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of five USDA SR Legacy analogues, assuming approximate equal mass contribution per component (20% each by weight): braised beef, cooked chicken feet, lotus root (blanched), kohlrabi (raw/pickled), and dry-roasted peanuts. Seasoning and sauce contributions to sodium are estimated conservatively. Values represent a plausible per-100g profile for the combined platter. Confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimates derived from analogous foods: lotus root (GI ~55, low-medium), kohlrabi (GI ~15, low), peanuts (GI ~14, low), braised beef (GI ~0, protein-dominant), chicken feet (GI ~0, protein-dominant). Composite weighted GI estimated at approximately 32.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this 5-component Beijing cold platter. GI estimated as a weighted composite: braised beef (~0, protein/fat dominant, no glycemic carbs), chicken feet (~0, collagen/protein dominant), lotus root (~55, starchy vegetable, University of Sydney GI Database), kohlrabi (~15, low-GI vegetable), dry-roasted peanuts (~14, University of Sydney GI Database item 1676). Weighted average across equal mass fractions yields approximately GI 32 (low category). GL per serving calculated as (32 × 9.4 × 400/100) / 100 = 11.9. Glycemic load per serving based on 400g serving size.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from braising sauces, marinades, and seasoning across five components
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size of this platter, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 480 mg per 100g (approximately 1920 mg per 400g serving) approaches or exceeds the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; request reduced-sauce preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low composite glycemic index (~32) but moderate carbohydrate content from lotus root and kohlrabi; peanuts contribute beneficial unsaturated fats
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low composite GI of approximately 32 makes this platter relatively favourable for blood glucose management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, but portion control is advised given the moderate carbohydrate load (~37.6g per 400g serving); the high protein and fat content from beef and chicken feet will further blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from braised beef and chicken feet skin; high sodium from sauces; beneficial unsaturated fats from peanuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the moderate saturated fat content (~2.6g per 100g) and high sodium load from this platter; the peanut component provides heart-beneficial monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats, but overall sodium intake from this dish warrants caution and should be balanced against daily cardiovascular dietary targets.

llm_training.natural_language_description: One Night in Beijing (5-Dish Platter), known in Chinese as 北京一夜（5拼）, is a curated assortment of five classic Old Beijing-style cold dishes served together as a single sharing platter. The five components are: (1) Old Beijing-style Sauce Beef — slow-braised beef in a soy and spice marinade, sliced thin and served cold with a savoury, umami-rich flavour; (2) Spicy Chicken Feet — marinated in chilli and aromatics, offering a chewy, gelatinous texture with a bold spicy kick; (3) Lotus Root — blanched or lightly pickled lotus root slices with a crisp, refreshing texture and mild earthy flavour; (4) Orange-Flavoured Cooling Kohlrabi — julienned kohlrabi dressed in a citrus-orange seasoning, providing a sweet, tangy, and cooling contrast; and (5) Colourful Peanuts — seasoned dry-roasted peanuts adding crunch, nuttiness, and a savoury-sweet finish. This platter is a hallmark of Beijing lěng pīn (cold appetiser) culture, typically served as a starter or accompaniment to drinks and main courses. Nutritionally, the platter is protein-rich from the beef and chicken feet, provides beneficial unsaturated fats from peanuts, and delivers dietary fibre and micronutrients from the vegetable components. The composite glycemic index is low (approximately 32), making it relatively suitable for blood glucose management. However, the sodium content is high due to the braising and marinating sauces, which is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains peanuts (a major allergen) and likely soy and gluten from the sauce components. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those requiring halal-certified food without specific confirmation from the restaurant.

confidence_reason: No single approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS) contains a direct entry for this specific 5-component Beijing cold platter as served by See Ya Tonight restaurant in Singapore. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of five USDA SR Legacy analogues with equal mass weighting. GI is estimated from analogous foods in the University of Sydney GI Database. The composite estimation introduces uncertainty, particularly for sodium (which depends heavily on sauce preparation) and fat (which depends on chicken feet skin retention). Medium confidence is appropriate.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates from five USDA SR Legacy analogues (braised beef, cooked chicken feet, lotus root, kohlrabi, peanuts) weighted equally at 20% each by mass. Actual values will vary significantly based on restaurant preparation, sauce quantities, and component ratios. 2. Sodium is estimated conservatively at 480 mg/100g; actual sodium may be substantially higher due to soy-based braising sauces and marinades. 3. GI is estimated at approximately 32 (low) based on weighted analogues from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this dish. 4. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not meaningfully reported across the USDA SR Legacy analogues for these components and is not a primary nutrient of concern for this dish type. 5. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1g/100g from the braised beef component; this is a trace level. 6. The dish contains peanuts (declared allergen) and likely soy and gluten from braising sauces; halal status is unconfirmed. 7. Serving size of 400g is an estimate for a shared platter; actual platter weight at See Ya Tonight restaurant has not been independently verified. 8. GL per serving = (32 × 9.4 × 400/100) / 100 = 11.9. 9. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are all estimated from USDA SR Legacy component analogues. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entries for these food components.
